title: "whatever you put your mind to"
summary: Why the 2007 Disney movie "Meet The Robinsons" is absolutely PEAK

sections.heading: Background
sections.body:
A couple months ago, my brother was back home from college on spring break and my family got the chance to come together to watch a movie in the living room one night. I've never been much of a movie watcher, and if you asked me now I probably couldn't recall the last 5 movies I've watched. (except for maybe [Obsession](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Obsession_(2025_film) which is decently popular now)

My mom was excited in particular, because we would be watching [Meet The Robinsons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Meet_the_Robinsons), a movie that she's always loved when since we were little, releasing around the time my brother was born.

At its core, the movie follows a young inventor named Lewis, who experiences failure after failure while searching for a place where he belongs. Through a series of increasingly bizarre adventures, he is repeatedly confronted with the same message:

`Keep Moving Forward.`

As a kid, that felt like a simple motivational slogan. But it feels more like a description of what learning takes.

Beneath the time travel, flying cars, and cartoon chaos is a surprisingly simple idea: the people who accomplish remarkable things are often just the people who kept going after everyone else would have quit.

sections.heading: You can do...
sections.body:
My parents used to tell me all the time (and they still do every so often) 

`"You can do anything you put your mind to".` 

However old I was, I remember it sounding silly, because to me there were some things that physically couldn't be done mind aside. 

But now, the spirit of those words resonate with me deeply. I like to hold the view that You, yes YOU 🫵🏾, the reader, can learn anything you put your mind to learning. And by extension do almost anything you put your mind to doing.

There is this engineer who makes reels on systems, computers, and programming named [Bahaa Tantaoui](https://www.instagram.com/bahae.tantaoui?igsh=MWp0NnpkdXR4ZWxqYw==). While I consider almost all of his content the opposite of brainrot, the one message that stuck out to me was something he said in his "thoughts" highlight on his page:
"A lot of learning happens when you let your curiousity run rampant and not contain it. No cirriculum, No "finish line". Just you chasing more answers to questions that keep poping up."

The reality of his words couldn't be more true. Through cirriculum, roadmaps and structure I gained loads of exposure and an idea in my head of a possible route or approach I could take. But the "concept finally clicking" and puzzle-piece-fitting experience of learning always happened during deep self research and hands on.

sections.heading: A "Different Human"
sections.body:
One of the biggest barriers to learning something difficult is the belief that the people who already know it are fundamentally different from you.

We've all experienced it.

You watch an developer explain a complex system. You see a researcher discuss a topic that took decades to develop. You watch an artist create something beautiful seemingly from nothing. 

And your first thought is, "They're cracked out of their mind" (Or mine is at least)

And to be fair, a bit of the friction is the fact that intellegence exists. Some people learn faster. Some have incredible memories. Some seem to genuienly operate on a different level than others.

But I think we often overestimate how much of expertise comes from raw talent and underestimate how much comes from time, persistence, and failure.

We don't see the hundreds of hours spent confused by documentation. We don't see the years of reasearch dead ends. We don't see the thousands of sketches that never left a notebook.

The type of learning that Bahaa describes, driven by curiosity rather than a curriculum, is 100% messy. It involves getting stuck. It involves being wrong over and over again. 

He describes it as recursive even, until you find your base cases.

sections.heading: A Mirror
sections.body:
Something that really stuck with me in the movie was how Lewis had to wrestle with failure.

When Lewis first tries to fix the time machine, he becomes consumed by the fact that he failed. He immediately jumps to the conclusion that because he couldn't solve the problem right away, he should give up entirely.

As a viewer, it's almost frustrating to watch. You just want to jump through the screen and shake him screaming, "Just try again!!!"

But I think that's exactly the point. The movie isn't showing us a character who is uniquely flawed. It's showing us ourselves.

There have been plenty of times I've looked at something difficult and decided it wasn't for me. 

I think the uncomfortable truth is that most of us don't quit because something is impossible. We quit because failure feels like evidence that we aren't capable. Lewis sees a failed invention and thinks it means he's a failure.

The Robinsons see a failed invention and think it's progress. That's the entire difference.

When I look back at the people we admire:
They usually aren't the people who never failed. They're the people who refused to interpret failure as the end of the story.

sections.heading: Wrap Up
sections.body:
I think what really made my mom enjoy the movie is that it could reinforce the type of mindset she wanted to instill in me, and evident by this write up I'd say it was at the least very influential.

There are thousands of takeaways you could pull out from this movie, but I think she put it best: 

`You CAN DO ANYTHING you put your mind to.`

So if there's something you've always wanted to learn or do, maybe the answer isn't to wait until you feel talented enough.

Maybe the answer is the same one Lewis spent the entire movie learning.

Maybe its corny or cheesy, but theres so much truth to it:

`Keep moving forward.`
